summary:
Tubular jade objects resembling the later cong form — rectangular on the outside but circular in bore — have been identified among the jade artifacts of the [[event:1526|Hongshan culture]] (4700–2900 BC) of northeastern China. These objects presage the fully developed cong form that would become one of the most iconic ritual objects of the Liangzhu culture further south. The Hongshan examples, though less standardized than later cong, reflect the same impulse to create geometrically complex forms from hard nephrite through grinding and drilling.

In later Chinese ritual contexts, the cong was associated with the earth and used in conjunction with the bi disc in ceremonies mediating between the human and cosmic realms. Whether the Hongshan predecessors carried equivalent meanings is uncertain, but their consistent presence in elite burial contexts suggests they held significant symbolic or spiritual value. The relationship between Hongshan and Liangzhu jade traditions remains an active area of research, with scholars debating whether there was direct cultural transmission or independent parallel development in the shared jade-working traditions of Neolithic China.
